title: PHP Basics
description: Master the fundamentals of PHP programming — from syntax to arrays.

sections.title: What is PHP?
sections.lesson:
PHP (PHP: Hypertext Preprocessor) is a server-side scripting language designed primarily for web development. PHP code runs on the server and generates HTML that is sent to the client's browser.

  • Free and open-source
  • Runs on all major platforms (Windows, Linux, macOS)
  • Works with most web servers (Apache, Nginx)
  • Integrates easily with databases like MySQL

A PHP file has the .php extension and can contain HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and PHP code.

<?php
  echo 'Hello, World!';
?>

questions.question: Where does PHP code run?
questions.explanation: PHP is a server-side language — the server executes it and sends the result (usually HTML) to the browser.

questions.question: What file extension do PHP files use?
questions.explanation: PHP files use the .php extension. The web server recognizes this extension and processes the file through the PHP engine.

sections.title: PHP Syntax & Tags
sections.lesson:
PHP code is placed inside special opening and closing tags that tell the server where PHP begins and ends.

<?php
  // Your PHP code here
?>

Key syntax rules:

  • Every PHP statement ends with a semicolon ;
  • PHP is not case-sensitive for keywords (echo, ECHO, Echo all work)
  • Variable names are case-sensitive ($name ≠ $Name)

You can write comments in PHP three ways:

<?php
  // Single-line comment
  # Also a single-line comment
  /* Multi-line
     comment */
  echo 'PHP is fun!'; // inline comment
?>

questions.question: What character ends every PHP statement?
questions.explanation: Every PHP statement must end with a semicolon (;). Missing it causes a parse error.

questions.question: Which of these is a valid single-line comment in PHP?
questions.explanation: PHP uses // for single-line comments (and # also works). <!-- --> is HTML comment syntax.

sections.title: Variables & Data Types
sections.lesson:
In PHP, a variable starts with the $ sign followed by the variable name. Variable names are case-sensitive.

<?php
  $name = 'Alice';    // String
  $age  = 25;         // Integer
  $gpa  = 3.75;       // Float
  $enrolled = true;   // Boolean
  $score = null;      // Null
?>

PHP is a loosely typed language — you don't declare the type, PHP figures it out automatically.

TypeExampleDescription
String'Hello'Text in single or double quotes
Integer42Whole numbers
Float3.14Numbers with decimals
Booleantrue / falseTrue or False
NullnullNo value assigned

questions.question: What symbol must start every PHP variable name?
questions.explanation: In PHP, all variables must begin with the dollar sign ($). For example: $name, $age, $total.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly declares a string variable in PHP?
questions.explanation: PHP variables are declared with $ and assigned with = (single equals). No type declaration is needed.

questions.question: PHP is considered a ________ typed language.
questions.explanation: PHP is loosely typed — you don't need to declare a variable's type. PHP infers it automatically from the value.

sections.title: Operators
sections.lesson:
PHP supports several types of operators for performing operations on values.

Arithmetic Operators:

<?php
  $a = 10; $b = 3;
  echo $a + $b;  // 13  (addition)
  echo $a - $b;  // 7   (subtraction)
  echo $a * $b;  // 30  (multiplication)
  echo $a / $b;  // 3.33 (division)
  echo $a % $b;  // 1   (modulo)
  echo $a ** $b; // 1000 (exponentiation)
?>

Comparison Operators:

OperatorMeaningExample
==Equal (loose)5 == '5' → true
===Identical (strict)5 === '5' → false
!=Not equal5 != 3 → true
>Greater than5 > 3 → true
<Less than3 < 5 → true

String Concatenation: Use the . (dot) operator to join strings.

<?php
  $first = 'Hello';
  $second = 'World';
  echo $first . ' ' . $second; // Hello World
?>

questions.question: Which operator is used to concatenate (join) strings in PHP?
questions.explanation: PHP uses the dot (.) operator to concatenate strings. Example: 'Hello' . ' World' produces 'Hello World'.

questions.question: What is the result of 10 % 3 in PHP?
questions.explanation: The % operator returns the remainder of division. 10 ÷ 3 = 3 remainder 1, so 10 % 3 = 1.

questions.question: What does the === operator check in PHP?
questions.explanation: === is the strict equality operator. It returns true only if both the value AND the data type are identical. == only compares values.

sections.title: String Functions
sections.lesson:
PHP provides many built-in functions to work with strings. Here are the most commonly used:

<?php
  $str = 'Hello, World!';

  echo strlen($str);           // 13 - string length
  echo strtoupper($str);       // HELLO, WORLD!
  echo strtolower($str);       // hello, world!
  echo str_replace('World', 'PHP', $str); // Hello, PHP!
  echo substr($str, 0, 5);     // Hello
  echo strpos($str, 'World');  // 7 (position)
  echo trim('  hello  ');      // 'hello' (removes spaces)
  echo str_word_count($str);   // 2
?>

String interpolation — embedding variables inside double-quoted strings:

<?php
  $name = 'Alice';
  echo "Hello, $name!";        // Hello, Alice!
  echo 'Hello, $name!';        // Hello, $name! (no interpolation in single quotes)
?>

questions.question: Which PHP function returns the length of a string?
questions.explanation: strlen() returns the number of characters in a string. Example: strlen('Hello') returns 5.

questions.question: What does substr('Hello World', 6) return?
questions.explanation: substr() extracts part of a string. Starting at index 6, 'Hello World' gives 'World'.

questions.question: Variable interpolation works inside ______ quoted strings.
questions.explanation: Variables inside double-quoted strings are automatically replaced with their values. Single-quoted strings treat $ literally.

sections.title: Conditional Statements
sections.lesson:
Conditional statements let your program make decisions based on conditions.

if / elseif / else:

<?php
  $score = 85;

  if ($score >= 90) {
      echo 'Grade: A';
  } elseif ($score >= 80) {
      echo 'Grade: B';
  } elseif ($score >= 70) {
      echo 'Grade: C';
  } else {
      echo 'Grade: F';
  }
  // Output: Grade: B
?>

switch statement — useful when comparing one variable against many values:

<?php
  $day = 'Monday';
  switch ($day) {
      case 'Monday':
          echo 'Start of work week';
          break;
      case 'Friday':
          echo 'End of work week';
          break;
      default:
          echo 'Midweek';
  }
?>

Ternary operator — a shorthand for simple if/else:

<?php
  $age = 20;
  $status = ($age >= 18) ? 'Adult' : 'Minor';
  echo $status; // Adult
?>

questions.question: Which keyword is used in PHP for an 'else if' condition?
questions.explanation: PHP uses elseif (one word) for chaining conditions. Note: 'else if' (two words) also works, but elseif is the standard PHP style.

questions.question: What is the output of: echo (5 > 3) ? 'Yes' : 'No';
questions.explanation: The ternary operator returns the first value ('Yes') if the condition is true, second value ('No') if false. 5 > 3 is true, so 'Yes' is returned.

questions.question: Which statement is best when checking one variable against many specific values?
questions.explanation: switch is designed for comparing a single variable against multiple possible values, making it more readable than a long if/elseif chain.

sections.title: Loops
sections.lesson:
Loops execute a block of code repeatedly. PHP has four main loop types:

for loop — when you know the exact number of iterations:

<?php
  for ($i = 1; $i <= 5; $i++) {
      echo $i . ' ';  // 1 2 3 4 5
  }
?>

while loop — repeats while a condition is true:

<?php
  $n = 1;
  while ($n <= 3) {
      echo $n . ' ';  // 1 2 3
      $n++;
  }
?>

foreach loop — iterates over each element in an array:

<?php
  $fruits = ['Apple', 'Banana', 'Cherry'];
  foreach ($fruits as $fruit) {
      echo $fruit . ' ';  // Apple Banana Cherry
  }
?>

Use break to exit a loop early, and continue to skip to the next iteration.

questions.question: Which loop is best suited for iterating over every element in an array?
questions.explanation: foreach is designed specifically for iterating over arrays and objects — it automatically handles the index and stopping condition.

questions.question: What does the break statement do inside a loop?
questions.explanation: break terminates the loop entirely and execution continues after the loop block. continue only skips the current iteration.

questions.question: In a for loop: for($i=0; $i<3; $i++), how many times does the loop body execute?
questions.explanation: The loop runs for $i = 0, 1, 2 (stops when $i reaches 3). That's 3 iterations total.

sections.title: Functions
sections.lesson:
A function is a reusable block of code that performs a specific task. Define functions with the function keyword.

<?php
  // Define a function
  function greet($name) {
      return 'Hello, ' . $name . '!';
  }

  // Call the function
  echo greet('Alice');  // Hello, Alice!
  echo greet('Bob');    // Hello, Bob!
?>

Default parameter values:

<?php
  function power($base, $exp = 2) {
      return $base ** $exp;
  }
  echo power(3);     // 9  (uses default exp=2)
  echo power(2, 10); // 1024
?>

Return values: Use return to send a value back to the caller. A function without return returns null.

Variable scope: Variables declared inside a function are local and not accessible outside it.

questions.question: Which keyword is used to define a function in PHP?
questions.explanation: PHP uses the 'function' keyword to define a function. Example: function myFunc() { ... }

questions.question: What does the return statement do in a PHP function?
choices: Sends a value back to the caller
questions.answer: Sends a value back to the caller
questions.explanation: return exits the function and optionally sends a value back to where the function was called. Without return, the function returns null.

questions.question: What are default parameter values used for?
questions.explanation: Default parameter values let you call a function without passing all arguments. The default is used when no argument is provided.

sections.title: Arrays
sections.lesson:
An array stores multiple values in a single variable. PHP supports indexed and associative arrays.

Indexed arrays (numeric keys starting at 0):

<?php
  $colors = ['Red', 'Green', 'Blue'];
  echo $colors[0]; // Red
  echo count($colors); // 3
?>

Associative arrays (named/string keys):

<?php
  $student = [
      'name'  => 'Alice',
      'grade' => 90,
      'major' => 'CS'
  ];
  echo $student['name'];  // Alice
  echo $student['grade']; // 90
?>

Common array functions:

<?php
  $nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9];
  sort($nums);                  // Sort ascending
  echo array_sum($nums);        // 23
  echo in_array(4, $nums);      // true (1)
  array_push($nums, 2);         // Add to end
  $last = array_pop($nums);     // Remove from end
?>

questions.question: What index does the first element of a PHP array have?
questions.explanation: PHP arrays (like most programming languages) are zero-indexed. The first element is at index 0, second at 1, etc.

questions.question: Which syntax creates an associative array in PHP?
questions.explanation: PHP uses => (fat arrow) to define key-value pairs in associative arrays. Example: ['name' => 'Alice'].

questions.question: Which function returns the number of elements in a PHP array?
questions.explanation: count() returns the number of elements in an array. Example: count([1, 2, 3]) returns 3.

sections.title: PHP Forms & User Input
sections.lesson:
PHP can collect user input from HTML forms. Forms use either GET or POST method.

HTML Form:

<form method='post' action='process.php'>
  <input type='text' name='username'>
  <input type='submit' value='Submit'>
</form>

Processing in PHP:

<?php
  if ($_SERVER['REQUEST_METHOD'] === 'POST') {
      $username = $_POST['username'];
      // Always sanitize user input!
      $safe = htmlspecialchars($username);
      echo 'Hello, ' . $safe;
  }
?>

GET vs POST:

FeatureGETPOST
Data locationURL query stringRequest body
Visible in URLYesNo
Data limit~2000 charsNo practical limit
Use forSearch, filtersLogin, forms

Security: Always use htmlspecialchars() to prevent XSS attacks when outputting user-provided data.

questions.question: Which superglobal is used to access POST form data in PHP?
questions.explanation: $_POST is the PHP superglobal array that contains data sent via HTTP POST method from HTML forms.

questions.question: Which function should you use to prevent XSS attacks when displaying user input?
questions.explanation: htmlspecialchars() converts special characters like < and > to HTML entities, preventing them from being interpreted as HTML/JavaScript.

questions.question: Which HTTP method sends data in the URL (visible to the user)?
questions.explanation: GET appends form data to the URL as a query string (e.g., ?name=Alice). POST sends data in the request body, keeping it out of the URL.
